turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument now number oh one six eighty-seven, the United States versus Leonard Cotton, et al. Mr. Dreeben. [Inaudible]

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. This case is typical of many Federal drug prosecutions that were tried before this Court's decision in Apprendi versus New Jersey. Respondents were indicted on a superseding indictment that alleged a conspiracy to distribute cocaine and cocaine base without alleging a specific threshold quantity of drugs that were involved in the offense. Respondents were convicted of that offense at trial, and the evidence established at trial that the offense involved well in excess of fifty grams of cocaine base, the threshold quantity of drugs to authorize a minimum sentence of ten years and a maximum sentence of life imprisonment. At sentencing, as all parties expected, the judge made findings of drug quantity and determined that the quantities of drugs involved in the offense justified a sentencing range up to life imprisonment and imposed sentences on several respondents of life imprisonment and others of thirty years' imprisonment. Respondents made no objection to the judge's procedure in determining drug quantity himself without a jury trial determination on that issue or...

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: without an allegation... Well at at...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: at sentencing , is it it's it's really not that much of a burden to just send it back to the judge and tell him to do it right.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: The problem in this case, Justice Kennedy, is that the court of appeals has held that the omission of a drug quantity allegation from the indictment is a jurisdictional error that always requires automatic correction on plain error review regardless of the strength of the evidence against respondents on the question or on whether respondents had notice that they would ha- face an increased sentence as a result of enhanced quantities of...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: drug...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: but in but in order to test that, I'm I'm just asking why i- it's not as if we have to have a new trial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: It's pro-

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: happens is there's a new sentencing hearing.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: What will happen if the this Court affirms the judgment of the court of appeals is that respondents will not be subject to the sentences that Congress authorized and that the evidence unequivocally showed in this case were justified.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Your real objection is that...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: this this th- are we really just arguing about retroactivity then?

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: In this case we're not arguing about retroactivity.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: enhanced sentence on...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: here, the the verdict of the jury corresponded to the indictment, I take it.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: conspiracy...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: that under Apprendi, the the quantity may become an element, in effect.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: came down.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: This case was tried...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: before Apprendi.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: tried after Apprendi came down, there might, in fact, be a notice problem I assume.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Yes, absolutely, Justice O'Connor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: base.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: for your position to prevail here, do we have to overrule Ex parte Bain or somehow set that aside, which seems to suggest that if it's not in the indictment, it's a jurisdictional problem?

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, Justice O'Connor, this Court already has overruled Ex parte Bain on its square holding, which is that the narrowing of an indictment is impermissible and deprives the court of jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: In United S-

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: government can surely also argue that Ex parte Bain by its terms doesn't apply...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: to this...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, that that is a difference from Ex parte Bain, Chief Justice Rehnquist.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: element.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: There can be an error but not a matter of not going to a matter of jurisdiction.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: jurisdictional.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: and because there was no right of direct appeal.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: error. And...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Dreeben, you would like us to make that clear, wouldn't you, because it isn't in our cases so far, that that kind of error, whatever it is, doesn't qualify as, quote, jurisdictional?

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Yes, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: There there are many instances where the Court has said that an error is that that a requirement, certain requirement, is mandatory and jurisdictional.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, m- jurisdictional has been appended as a label to a variety of kinds of errors, but the relevant sense in which it's being invoked in this case and in which the lower court understood it was a kind of defect that may be raised at any time regardless of an objection and that is tantamount to subject matter jurisdiction, the sort of error that is so fundamental to the proceedings that harmless error review and plain error review simply don't apply.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: is just...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS

turns.text: In in the...

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: the time they filed the prior indictment, but when they withdraw it and file a se- se- second indictment, you normally would think they've withdrawn the charges that have been withdrawn.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, not in view of the fact that at the l- prevailing legal regime at the time...

turns.text: I I would.

turns.text: It didn't withdraw all references to quantity, Justice Stevens.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS

turns.text: And that's correct.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: part of this...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: just interrupt you by saying I think it would be most unfortunate if we decided this case on the particular fact that there was an original indictment and not and a superseding indictment.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, I I don't think it would mean n- nothing, Justice Stevens, but we are asking the Court to rule on the broader ground that when an indictment fails to allege what we now understand to be an element of the offense, but the evidence is sufficiently powerful so that any rational grand jury, if asked, would have found that.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: What what does the what does the government's position do to the Stirone case?

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Nothing, Just- Chief Justice Rehnquist, because Stirone was a case in which two features are present that are not present here.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Can you tell me, post-Apprendi in the trial courts, can the defendant agree with the government to plead guilty but leave it to the judge to determine the amounts of the drug involved?

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: It's a little unclear, Justice Kennedy, whether the defendant can do that because typically the government has not acquiesced and the courts have not been hospitable to partial pleas of guilty.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: And what does the government do when it indicts? There's there's no stipulation of any kind.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: No, Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: lesser included offense.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: I guess part of the problem in this case is that if we say that defendants are entitled to the benefit of a change in the law before their conviction becomes final, there's not much you can do because you'd have to issue a superseding indictment and you can't do that.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: We can't do it in this case, Justice Kennedy, which is why, in effect, the result that the court of appeals achieved is a tremendous windfall for the defendants.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: I don't know they'd object.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, the the objection could take place at trial when a defendant could have said the government has to prove this quantity up to the jury or it...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: could...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: I mean the the point at which the the the the failure to indict and allege on quantity becomes objectionable is at sentencing.

turns.text: Well, that was...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: no reason to.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: That was just what I was about to say, Justice Souter.

turns.text: you cannot reindict, but the objection would be irrelevant to that.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY

I I understand it's not this case, but would the Govern- what what would the government's position be if the evidence of quantity came out after the trial was concluded just as a result of a pre-sentence investigation and then a finding by the judge? What would what should happen in that kind of case?

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: I- In that kind of case, our position would be the same, that particularly on plain error review, the Court should look to the...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Mr. Dreeben,

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Go ahead...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I I'm I'm not sure I I y- you you say we should determine whether substantial rights has have been affected by by asking whether if the procedure that has been omitted had not been omitted, he would have been conv- he would have been convicted anyway.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: No, it's not the position that we're taking, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I don't...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: my point is it seems to me the way you decide whether substantial rights have been affected is not to ask the question would he b- would he have been convicted anyway.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.text: And those cases are called structural error cases.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, that's all the Court needs to hold in order to conclude that the court of appeals erred in this case because the court of appeals in this case got to the fourth step of plain error review, after finding an effect on substantial rights, and then it held that we really can't say what the grand jury would have done.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Any...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: can I just give you sort of an intermediate hypothetical? Supposing all the evidence of quantity developed after the grand jury had returned its indictment that it developed, that in the plea bargaining they found out how much dru- there really was involved, you'd treat that as the same case even though the gan- grand jury could not have indicted.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Well, i- i- if that case took place, as this one did, in a legal regime in which the government didn't believe it had to get a grand jury indictment on the point, then I suppose my answer to that is if we had known, we could have gone back to the grand jury and gotten...

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: indictment.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: And and that's what's odd about this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: there's nothing...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: except that the defendant doesn't have to proceed on any theory.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Of course, if we see it your way, it would be open to prosecutors all the time simply to make the grand jury proceeding a short-cut and not bother to get into quantity and, hence, not, in in effect, ad- advise the or the the the the grand jury that it's that it's going for something that might have the the potential for life.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Y- You could make that argument, Justice Souter, but in a post imb- Apprendi environment, Apprendi is a sufficiently well-known decision of this Court that no prosecutor would responsibly go to sentencing and say I would like to have an increased sentence regardless of the fact that we didn't charge drug quantity.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: court.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: in this case? If if the standard were harmless error rather than plain error, w- would you maintain that the government should still prevail?

turns.text: Yes, Justice Ginsburg, we would because of reasoning analogous to the Court used in Neder versus United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But the Court could well conclude otherwise were the test harmless error and you could still prevail.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: I think my point was that Bain involved a situation where the verdict and the indictment didn't correspond, and that a rule that says that's jurisdictional might not extend to this situation.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: specific offense.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That it --

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: I but I I think you have to recognize that the Court at that time, because there was no direct appeal, w- kind of expanded the concept of jurisdiction to reach constitutional errors.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: And I think that is exactly what the Apprendi Court and the majority is restricting now is is that that exact function of the Court.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: would result in...

turns.text: I mean, Apprendi was was based on, I assume, the long common law tradition.

I'm thinking back in the old days when there was jury discrimination and the courts routun- routinely -tinely said no, but many defense attorneys, knowing they were going to lose on it, made it for the record anyway, hoping that their case on appeal would be the one that changed the law?

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: from now.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: So, I don't think that I'm sure that there's attorneys throughout the country who were making these types of objections, sensing the change from McMillan and Almandarez-Torres, sensing the direction of this Court, but we we did not.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: That's I I ta- I'll take that as given.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: Justice Breyer, my my answer is that it all goes back to the Indictment Clause of the Fifth Amendment and to the jurisdictional end-run that Justice Souter was alluding to.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: All right but we've faced many, many cases in which the normal tendency of the courts has been to say, forget about errors at the grand jury stage.

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: function of the jur- of the of the petty jury?

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: do you mean when you say waives jurisdiction?

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: I don't mean waive jurisdiction, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.name: UNKNOWN

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: s- the rule of law I mean, sometimes you you roll the dice and sometimes the defense wins and oftentimes the government wins, Justice Kennedy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But in this case...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Sullivan, in light of the Johnson case and the Neder case, I think the government has a very strong argument here.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: Judge Blake instructed the jury, the petit jury, that drug quantity was not a concern of the court I mean, a con- concern of the jury.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Un- under the first indictment, would the jury have been instructed that it had to find the amounts?

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: I think in pre-Apprendi practice, no.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: You said that some people might differ about this particular case and maybe this time it's roll of the dice.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, I had the Olano test is quite clear that you don't l- well, that a decision on a basic right that is forfeited doesn't matter on if somebody is innocent or guilty.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: they're charged with.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: case, in in a case where the the law was was in flux, this would not be a determination a plain error determination wouldn't be that routinely this kind of omission could occur.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: I think this is the exception rather than the rule.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I thought that the the lack of conformity between the indictment and the conviction is not in this case.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Th- It's...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: be indicted for one offense and convicted of another.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I'd als-

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: the defense?

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: What was the defense at trial?

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: Justice Breyer, in this multi-defendant eight forty-six conspiracy, the defense was one part that the government cooperators lacked credibility, that they weren't reliable.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I mean, was it basically they didn't do it?

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: It was that and there was also multiple conspiracies.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: multiple...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: wonder on the harmless part or whether it's harmful.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: I I don't think that that that that that would've been you never use drug quantities as a defense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: You'd ra-

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: that's that that you see the reason...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: the reason...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: that I say that is because they're saying, look, this error is harmless, and part of the strength of your claim I think is that they never could've thought of it at the trial before Apprendi to raise it.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: I don't think it can be harmless, Justice Breyer, because the very thrust of Neder, the very thrust of harmless error analysis is that the government must prove beyond a reasonable doubt that the error didn't affect substantial rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: case because...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: in Neder, didn't the Court assume that substantial rights were affected?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I think in in either Neder or Johnson...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: it did.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: Tha- i- i- I think you're obviously correct, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: functions.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I was trying to a- direct your attention to, which is and and maybe this doesn't help you.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: And my point is , Justice Breyer, is that I agree with the premise of your question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: before the grand jury.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: if you're right in that, then Mechanik was wrongly decided.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: I I I don't believe that Mechanik was wrongly decided, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And you ha-

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: But i- i- it it's said that, you know, y- y- you can't attack the indictment rendered by the grand jury even though you might have some some reason to do so.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: That's correct, but there was never a challenge to the validity of the indictment the indictment itself in Mechanik, which is the root of our contention here.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: May I go back to your argument th- that you made a second ago that we never know what the grand jury would've done if it had been presented with the evidence? The difficulty that I have with that argument is, number one, I think we have a pretty clear body of law that tells the grand jury what its duty is, if it is presented with evidence which would justify an indictment with respect to quantity and hence the severity of the crime.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: Justice Souter, I think the answer is that we try to remain as true as we can to the Framers' intent and the Framers' fear of a corrupt judiciary or an o- an oppressive prosecutor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, wasn't that a fear...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Go ahead David.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Wasn't wasn't that a fear, in effect, that grand juries are simply going to be puppets that are going to be indicting without regard to evidence? Whereas, here the assumption is the evidence is overwhelming, and so that policy of wanting the grand jury to stand between the state and the individual is not really a policy that's implicated here.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: I I I yo- y- you're right in that regard, Justice Souter, but we don't know if the government did its duty and presented to this grand jury drug quantities in the superseding indictments.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: encourage it.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: tend not to nullify, I think, except when there are political considerations that the grand jury sort of smells in the circumstances.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: And I and I I guess it goes back, Justice Souter, to where I began this morning.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: with.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: think you've I think you got a good argument there except for the fact that we've got to find a distinction between the role of the grand juries and the petty juries given the fact that Neder is is on the record.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: The dilemma is, i- Justice Souter is that what this would crack open the gate to allow, I would suggest, the government to trample into the the grand jury function.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: charge one thing...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: the the verdict corresponded to the indictment.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: That -- No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: This case is jusst...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you you you don't agree that the verdict corresponded to the indictment, do you? I I thought the only reason that that issue was not in this case is because of Neder.

turns.name: TIMOTHY J. SULLIVAN
turns.text: That's correct, but it's also correct that I told the Chief Justice earlier that that the that the problem is a sentencing problem in this case and not a difference between what is the differ- My time is up.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Dreeben, you have one minute remaining.

turns.name: MICHAEL R. DREEBEN
turns.text: Unless the Court has any questions, the government waives rebuttal.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well.
